planting.note: Start indoors 6 to 4 weeks before the last frost and transplant after all danger of frost has passed.

directions: This indeterminate heirloom produces heavy clusters of small, bright yellow pear-shaped fruits with a mild, tangy flavor. Provide sturdy caging or staking, since vines sprawl vigorously all season. Pinch suckers if you want a more manageable plant and pick fruit regularly to keep production going. Water consistently at the base to avoid cracking and blossom-end rot.

planting.note: Direct sow 1 to 2 weeks after the last frost once soil is thoroughly warm.

directions: This compact, early-maturing heirloom yields round 5 to 7 pound melons with sweet yellow flesh, ideal for small gardens and containers. Sow in warm soil and give vines room to run along the ground or a low trellis with fruit support. Water deeply but reduce moisture as fruits ripen to concentrate sweetness. Harvest when the tendril nearest the fruit browns and the ground spot turns creamy.

planting.note: Start indoors 10 to 8 weeks before the last frost and transplant while weather is still cool.

directions: This heirloom long-day variety forms large, golden globe bulbs with a mild, sweet flavor, best suited to northern gardens with long summer days. Start seed early indoors and transplant seedlings while cool weather encourages leafy growth before bulbing. Keep beds weed-free and evenly moist, then taper off water as tops fall over. Cure the harvested bulbs in a dry, airy spot before storage.

planting.note: Direct sow around the last frost, or sprout indoors year-round for microgreens and sprouts.

directions: Alfalfa is a nutrient-rich legume grown for tender sprouts, microgreens, and nutritious greens as well as for soil improvement. For sprouts, rinse seed twice daily in a jar and harvest in 4 to 6 days once the shoots green up. In the garden, sow into warm, well-drained soil and cut greens young for the best flavor. Its deep taproot fixes nitrogen and draws up minerals, making it a useful companion in the garden.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall or early spring; cold stratification greatly improves germination.

directions: Angelica is a stately biennial with aromatic foliage and large umbels of small greenish-white flowers, prized in cooking and traditional herbalism. Seed needs light and cold to germinate, so sow fresh on the soil surface in fall or stratify before spring sowing. Give it rich, moist soil and part shade to mimic its native streamside habitat. It flowers and sets seed in its second year, then usually dies, so let some seed self-sow.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost; anise resents transplanting due to its taproot.

directions: Anise is an aromatic annual grown for its sweet, licorice-flavored seeds and feathery lower leaves. Sow directly where it will grow, as the taproot dislikes disturbance, and thin seedlings to give each plant room. It needs a long, warm season of about 100 to 120 days for seeds to ripen fully. Harvest the seed heads when they turn grayish-brown and dry them before threshing.

planting.note: Direct sow around the last frost; it self-sows readily once established.

directions: Black cumin is an easy, drought-tolerant annual with delicate blue flowers followed by seed pods full of pungent black seeds used in cooking and traditional wellness. Direct sow into well-drained soil in full sun, as it dislikes root disturbance. The ornamental flowers attract pollinators before giving way to inflated seed capsules. Let pods dry on the plant, then crush them to release the peppery, aromatic seeds.

planting.note: Direct sow into deep, loose soil after the last frost; the long taproot resists transplanting.

directions: Burdock, also called gobo, is a vigorous biennial grown for its long, slender edible roots and traditional herbal uses. Sow directly in deep, loose, stone-free soil so the roots can grow straight and long. Harvest the tender first-year roots in fall before they turn woody in the second year. Keep the bed weeded and evenly watered to encourage crisp, mild-flavored roots.

planting.note: Sow around the last frost; cold stratification improves germination of this hardy perennial.

directions: Catnip is a hardy perennial mint with fragrant gray-green leaves that delight cats and draw bees and butterflies. Sow seed shallowly in spring after a brief chill period, then thin to give plants room to bush out. It tolerates poor, dry soil and full sun, spreading readily if not contained. Cut back after flowering to keep plants tidy and encourage a fresh flush of aromatic foliage.

planting.note: Sow around the last frost; cold stratification aids germination of this hardy perennial.

directions: Chinese skullcap, or Huang Qin, is a hardy perennial with violet-blue tubular flowers, long valued in traditional East Asian herbalism. Sow seed after a period of cold stratification into well-drained soil in full sun. Roots are typically harvested from plants three or more years old, after the tops die back. Keep soil moderately moist and avoid waterlogging, which can rot the crown.

planting.note: Direct sow in cool weather and make successive sowings, since it bolts in summer heat.

directions: This open-pollinated cilantro produces lush leafy greens and, when it bolts, aromatic coriander seed. Sow directly in cool weather since it bolts quickly once temperatures climb. Make small successive sowings every two to three weeks for a steady leaf harvest. Allow a few plants to flower and set seed to collect coriander or let them self-sow for the next round.

planting.note: Sow around the last frost; this biennial to short-lived perennial blooms in its second year.

directions: Clary sage is an aromatic biennial or short-lived perennial with soft gray-green foliage and tall spikes of white, pink, or lilac flowers loved by pollinators. It thrives in poor, rocky, well-drained soil and full sun, tolerating drought once established. Sow seed shallowly and expect flowering in the second year. Deadhead spent spikes or leave some to self-sow for a continuous stand.

planting.note: Sow around the last frost; germination is slow and benefits from cold stratification.

directions: Comfrey is a vigorous, deep-rooted perennial with large leaves and bell-shaped flowers, valued for soil enrichment, mulch, and traditional external herbal uses. Seed germinates slowly and unevenly, so be patient and provide cool, moist conditions. Once established the taproot is nearly impossible to remove, so choose a permanent spot. Cut leaves several times a season to use as a nutrient-rich mulch or compost activator.

planting.note: Sow in early spring around the last frost; clumps return reliably each year.

directions: Common chives are a hardy perennial allium forming grassy clumps with a mild onion flavor and edible lavender-pink flowers. Sow seed in clusters and thin lightly, since chives grow happily in tight bunches. Snip leaves from the outside of the clump throughout the season and they will regrow. Divide crowded clumps every few years to keep them vigorous and productive.

planting.note: Direct sow around the last frost; the taproot dislikes transplanting.

directions: This dwarf dill stays short and bushy, making it ideal for containers, raised beds, and small gardens while delivering classic dill flavor. Sow directly where it will grow, as dill resents root disturbance. Harvest the ferny foliage as needed and let some plants flower for pickling heads and seed. Make successive sowings for a continuous supply of fresh leaves through the season.

planting.note: Surface sow around the last frost; seed is tiny and needs light to germinate.

directions: Creeping thyme forms a low, fragrant evergreen mat covered in purple-lavender blooms that draw bees and butterflies. Surface sow the tiny seed and press it into the soil without covering, since it needs light to germinate. It thrives in lean, well-drained soil and tolerates foot traffic, making it excellent between pavers and in rock gardens. Trim after flowering to keep the mat dense and tidy.

planting.note: Sow around the last frost; plants establish over the first season and overwinter as woody perennials.

directions: Culinary sage is a woody perennial with dusty gray-green leaves and a savory, aromatic flavor essential in the kitchen. Grow it in full sun and well-drained soil, watering sparingly once established since it dislikes wet feet. Harvest leaves lightly the first year and more freely in following seasons. Prune in spring to remove dead wood and keep the plant compact and productive.

planting.note: Direct sow in cool weather; it bolts quickly in heat, so grow in spring and fall.

directions: Curled chervil, sometimes called French parsley, is a delicate cool-season herb with lacy leaves and a subtle anise flavor. Sow directly in cool weather and part shade, as it bolts rapidly in summer heat. Make successive sowings in spring and again in late summer for fall harvests. Snip the tender leaves young and use them fresh, since their flavor fades when dried or cooked long.

planting.note: Sow in early spring; soaking seed overnight speeds notoriously slow germination.

directions: Curly parsley is a hardy biennial grown as an annual for its bright green, frilled leaves used as garnish and seasoning. Germination is slow, so soak seed overnight and keep the soil consistently moist. Harvest outer stems first and the plant will keep producing from the center. In milder zones it overwinters and flowers the second year, at which point flavor declines.

planting.note: Direct sow around the last frost; the taproot resents transplanting.

directions: Classic dill is an aromatic annual grown for both its feathery foliage and flavorful seed, a staple for pickling. Sow directly where it will grow, since the taproot dislikes disturbance, and provide shelter from strong wind for the tall stems. Harvest leaves anytime and collect seed heads once they turn brown. Let a few plants go to seed and they will self-sow for future crops.

planting.note: Sow around the last frost; cold stratification improves germination of this tall perennial.

directions: Elecampane is a bold perennial reaching several feet tall, with sunny yellow daisy-like flowers and aromatic roots used in traditional herbalism and natural dyes. Sow seed after cold stratification into moist, fertile soil in full sun to part shade. Give it plenty of space, as mature clumps grow large and stately. Roots are typically dug in fall of the second or third year for herbal use.

planting.note: Start indoors early after cold stratification; seedlings are slow, so give them a long head start.

directions: English lavender is a fragrant, hardy perennial with silvery foliage and purple flower spikes prized for their sweet aroma and pollinator appeal. Seed germinates slowly and benefits from cold stratification, so start indoors well ahead of transplanting. Grow in lean, sharply drained soil in full sun and avoid overwatering. Prune lightly after bloom to maintain a compact mound and prolong the plant's life.

planting.note: Surface sow around the last frost; seed needs light to germinate.

directions: Feverfew is a cheerful short-lived perennial producing masses of small white daisy-like flowers with yellow centers above aromatic foliage. Surface sow the seed and press it into the soil, since light aids germination. It grows easily in full sun to part shade and self-sows freely once established. Deadhead to prolong bloom and lift some volunteer seedlings each spring to keep a fresh stand.

planting.note: Direct sow in cool weather, or grow indoors year-round as sprouts and microgreens.

directions: Garden cress is one of the fastest herbs to grow, germinating in days and offering a zesty, peppery flavor for salads and sandwiches. Sow thickly on a moist surface for microgreens indoors, or direct sow in cool weather outdoors. Harvest young shoots within a couple of weeks before they turn tough or bolt. Make frequent small sowings for a steady supply of tender, spicy greens.

planting.note: Sow in early spring; clumps return each year and produce edible white flowers.

directions: Garlic chives are a hardy perennial allium with flat, grass-like leaves that carry a mild garlic flavor, plus fragrant edible white flowers late in the season. Sow seed in clumps and harvest leaves by snipping from the outside as needed. The star-shaped blooms attract pollinators but self-sow prolifically, so deadhead if you want to limit spread. Divide crowded clumps every few years to keep them vigorous.

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after the last frost once soil and nights are warm.

directions: Genovese basil is the classic Italian pesto basil, prized for its large, tender, deeply fragrant green leaves. Sow once soil is warm and keep plants well watered in full sun. Pinch the growing tips regularly and remove flower buds to encourage bushy growth and prevent the leaves from turning bitter. Harvest often, as frequent picking keeps the plant producing until frost.

planting.note: Surface sow around the last frost; the tiny seed needs light to germinate.

directions: German chamomile is a fast-growing annual with delicate white daisy flowers and a sweet, apple-like fragrance, popular for herbal tea. Surface sow the fine seed and press it into the soil, since light aids germination. Harvest the flower heads when petals are fully open and dry them for tea. It self-sows readily, so let a few flowers mature to reseed the patch.

planting.note: Sow in early spring; soaking seed overnight speeds slow germination.

directions: Giant of Italy is a vigorous flat-leaf Italian parsley with large, aromatic dark green leaves and a robust, authentic flavor. Germination is slow, so soak seed overnight and keep the soil consistently moist. Harvest outer stems first and the plant keeps producing from the center all season. This hardy biennial often overwinters in milder zones, flowering and setting seed in its second year.

planting.note: Start indoors 6 to 4 weeks before the last frost and transplant once nights are warm.

directions: Holy basil, or tulsi, is an aromatic tender annual revered in Ayurvedic tradition and grown for fragrant leaves used in tea and wellness blends. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant only after nights stay warm, as it is frost-sensitive. Pinch flower spikes to prolong leafy growth, or leave some blooms for pollinators and self-seeding. Harvest leaves and flowering tops regularly and dry them for tea.

planting.note: Sow around the last frost; this hardy perennial establishes over the first season.

directions: Hyssop is a fragrant, semi-evergreen perennial herb with narrow leaves and spikes of blue flowers that attract bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds. It thrives in full sun and well-drained soil and tolerates drought once established. Sow seed shallowly and thin to give plants room to form a bushy shrub. Trim after flowering to keep the plant compact and encourage fresh aromatic growth.
